[mpiexec@pvc-s-28] HYD_bstrap_setup (../../../../../src/pm/i_hydra/libhydra/bstrap/src/intel/i_hydra_bstrap.c:1063): error waiting for event
Could you please advise me how I can resolve this issue? When using an interactive node launched by the command srun --partition=pvc9 -n 1 -c 8 --gres=gpu:1 --nodes=1 --account=airr-p15-dawn-gpu --pty bash, everything worked fine for me.
Thanks a lot for your help.
Best,
Wei-Tse

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: MPI / Slurm Environment / Dawn (PVC9)
Technical Action Required: No (user-side environment correction)
Escalation Required: No (unless issue persists after environment fix)
Urgency: Medium (job fails in batch mode)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user reports an MPI bootstrap failure when running:

    mpirun -np 1 gmx_mpi -h

within a batch job on the pvc9 partition.

Error excerpt:
    HYD_spawn ... execvp error on file /usr/bin/srun (No such file or directory)

Key observations:
- The job works correctly in an interactive session launched via `srun`.
- The batch script manually purges modules and loads a custom module path.
- The error indicates Intel MPI’s Hydra bootstrap cannot locate `srun`.

Most likely cause:
The batch script is not loading the correct Dawn node environment (RHEL9 default stack), so Slurm integration and related paths (including srun) are not correctly initialised inside the job environment.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Ensure correct base environment is loaded
   Instruct user to add:

       module load rhel9/default-dawn

   immediately after `module purge` in their submission script.

   This ensures:
   - Correct RHEL9 runtime environment
   - Proper Slurm integration
   - Required system paths (including srun)
   - Compatible MPI environment

2) Recommend simplified module order
   Example structure:

       module purge
       module load rhel9/default-dawn
       module use /usr/local/dawn/software/spack-rocky9/spack-modules/dawn-2025-03-23/linux-rocky9-sapphirerapids
       module load gromacs/2024.5

   Avoid mixing incompatible stacks unless required.

3) Retest with minimal command
   After modification, test again with:

       mpirun -np 1 gmx_mpi -h

4) If issue persists, request:
   - Full submission script
   - Complete stdout and stderr files
   - Output of:
         which mpirun
         which srun
         module list

EXPECTED SIGNAL AFTER FIX
- No Hydra bootstrap error.
- mpirun executes successfully.
- gmx_mpi help output prints normally.

BRANCH: If still failing
- Confirm Intel MPI is correctly integrated with Slurm.
- Verify that `srun` exists in PATH within job:
      echo $PATH
      ls -l /usr/bin/srun
- Escalate to platform admins only if Slurm binary is genuinely unavailable on compute node (unlikely).

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: MPI-related runtime error on pvc9

Hello Wei-Tse,

It looks like the Dawn node environment is not being fully initialised in your batch job.

Please add the following line after `module purge` in your submission script:

  module load rhel9/default-dawn

This loads the correct RHEL9 runtime and Slurm integration required for MPI jobs on pvc9.

After adding this, please resubmit the job and let us know if the issue persists. If so, please send the full submission script and output files.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not suggest installing MPI manually.
- Do not recommend modifying system Slurm paths.
- Avoid blaming firewall or host availability unless independently verified.
- Keep troubleshooting limited to supported module stacks.

'tickets': 1.0}}, 'text': '# GROMACS[¶](#gromacs "Permalink to this headline")

Gromacs describes itself at <http://www.gromacs.org>

> GROMACS is a versatile package to perform molecular dynamics, i.e. simulate the Newtonian equations of motion for systems with hundreds to millions of particles.

## Running GROMACS on CSD3[¶](#running-gromacs-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

GROMACS is supported on each of the hardware partitions on CSD3.

To load the most recent build of GROMACS, first load the partition default environment e.g. (for the ampere partition):

```
module purge && module load rhel8/default-amp
```

and then do:

```
module load gromacs
```

which will make available the gmx\_mpi front-end as well as various mdrun binaries.

A sample job script to run GROMACS:

```
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --account CHANGEME
#SBATCH --partition cclake
#SBATCH --nodes 2
#SBATCH --ntasks 28
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task 4 # 2 full cclake nodes ==  112 cpus, split into 28 MPI tasks with 4 OpenMP thread  per task (cpus-per-task)
#SBATCH --time 02:00:00
#SBATCH --output gromacs.out
#SBATCH --error gromacs.err

module purge
module load rhel8/cclake/base gromacs/2024.4/gcc/intel-oneapi-mpi/wnncn7o4

# Running Intro tutorial (https://tutorials.gromacs.org/docs/md-intro-tutorial.html)
# Comment out the following (except the last line) to use your own setup.
wget https://gitlab.com/gromacs/online-tutorials/md-intro-tutorial/-/archive/main/md-intro-tutorial-main.zip
unzip md-intro-tutorial-main.zip
cd md-intro-tutorial-main/data
grep -v HETATM input/1fjs.pdb > 1fjs_protein_tmp.pdb

### Jobs requiring MPI[¶](#jobs-requiring-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

We currently recommend using the [Intel MPI Library](https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/tools/oneapi/mpi-library.html) provided by the oneAPI toolkit:

```
module av intel-oneapi-mpi
```

To use GPU Aware MPI and allow passing device buffers to MPI calls, set the `I_MPI_OFFLOAD`
environment variable to `1` in your submission script:

```
export I_MPI_OFFLOAD=1
```

If you are sure that your code only involves buffers of the same type (e.g.
only GPU buffers or only host buffers) in a single
MPI operation you can further optimize MPI communication between GPUs by setting

```
export I_MPI_OFFLOAD_SYMMETRIC=1
```

This will disable handling of MPI communication between GPU buffers and host buffers.

### Multithreading jobs[¶](#multithreading-jobs "Permalink to this headline")

If your code uses multithreading (e.g. host-based OpenMP), you will need to
specify the number of threads per process in your Slurm batch script using the
cores-per-task parameter. For example, to run a hybrid MPI-OpenMP application
using 24 processes and 4 threads per task:

```
#SBATCH -n 24 # or --ntasks
#SBATCH -c 4 # or --cores-per-task
```

If you do *not* specify the cores-per-task parameter Slurm will pin the
threads to the same core, reducing performance.

## Compiling[¶](#compiling "Permalink to this headline")

Note that the `default-X` module, which is loaded by default on nodes of type X, arranges for `mpicc`, `mpif90` etc to be found and to use the recommended compilers and MPI implementation automatically when invoked. These wrapper commands supply the correct flags for compiling with the particular MPI implementation to which they belong.

When compiling code, it is usually possible to remove any direct MPI library references in your Makefile as mpicc & mpif90 will take care of these details. In the Makefile, simply set

```
CC=mpicc
```

etc, or define

```
export CC=mpicc
```

etc in the shell before compilation.

If some required libraries are missing, please let us know and we can try to install them centrally (as a module).

#!/bin/bash
#!
#! Example SLURM job script for Wilkes3 (AMD EPYC 7763, ConnectX-6, A100)
#! Last updated: Fri 30 Jul 11:07:58 BST 2021
#!
#!#############################################################
#!#### Modify the options in this section as appropriate ######
#!#############################################################
#! sbatch directives begin here ###############################
#! Name of the job:
#SBATCH -J smolvla_mikasa
#! Which project should be charged (NB Wilkes2 projects end in '-GPU'):
#SBATCH -A T2-CS178-GPU
#! How many whole nodes should be allocated?
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#! How many (MPI) tasks will there be in total?
#! Note probably this should not exceed the total number of GPUs in use.
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#! Specify the number of GPUs per node (between 1 and 4; must be 4 if nodes>1).
#! Note that the job submission script will enforce no more than 32 cpus per GPU.
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#! How much wallclock time will be required?
#SBATCH --time=36:00:00
#! What types of email messages do you wish to receive?
#SBATCH --mail-type=ALL
#! Uncomment this to prevent the job from being requeued (e.g. if
#! interrupted by node failure or system downtime):
#SBATCH --no-requeue
#! Do not change:
#SBATCH -p ampere
#! sbatch directives end here (put any additional directives above this line)
#! Notes:
#! Charging is determined by GPU number*walltime.
#! Number of nodes and tasks per node allocated by SLURM (do not change):
numnodes=$SLURM_JOB_NUM_NODES
numtasks=$SLURM_NTASKS
mpi_tasks_per_node=$(echo "$SLURM_TASKS_PER_NODE" | sed -e 's/^([0-9][0-9]).$/\1/')
#! ############################################################
#! Modify the settings below to specify the application's environment, location
#! and launch method:
#! Optionally modify the environment seen by the application
#! (note that SLURM reproduces the environment at submission irrespective of ~/.bashrc):
. /etc/profile.d/modules.sh # Leave this line (enables the module command)
module purge # Removes all modules still loaded
module load rhel8/default-amp # REQUIRED - loads the basic environment
#! Insert additional module load commands after this line if needed:
#! Full path to application executable:
nvidia-smi
application="./convert.sh"
#! Run options for the application:
options=""
#! Work directory (i.e. where the job will run):
workdir="$SLURM_SUBMIT_DIR" # The value of SLURM_SUBMIT_DIR sets workdir to the directory
1. in which sbatch is run.
#! Are you using OpenMP (NB this is unrelated to OpenMPI)? If so increase this
#! safe value to no more than 128:
export OMP_NUM_THREADS=1
#! Number of MPI tasks to be started by the application per node and in total (do not change):
np=$[${numnodes}*${mpi_tasks_per_node}]
#! Choose this for a pure shared-memory OpenMP parallel program on a single node:
#! (OMP_NUM_THREADS threads will be created):
CMD="$application $options"
#! Choose this for a MPI code using OpenMPI:
#CMD="mpirun -npernode $mpi_tasks_per_node -np $np $application $options"
###############################################################
1. 1. 1. You should not have to change anything below this line ####
###############################################################
cd $workdir
echo -e "Changed directory to `pwd`.\n"
JOBID=$SLURM_JOB_ID
echo -e "JobID: $JOBID\n======"
echo "Time: `date`"
echo "Running on master node: `hostname`"
echo "Current directory: `pwd`"
if [ "$SLURM_JOB_NODELIST" ]; then
#! Create a machine file:
export NODEFILE=`generate_pbs_nodefile`
cat $NODEFILE | uniq > archive_logs/machine.file.$JOBID
echo -e "\nNodes allocated:\n================"
echo `cat archive_logs/machine.file.$JOBID | sed -e 's/\..*$//g'`
fi
echo -e "\nnumtasks=$numtasks, numnodes=$numnodes, mpi_tasks_per_node=$mpi_tasks_per_node (OMP_NUM_THREADS=$OMP_NUM_THREADS)"
echo -e "\nExecuting command:\n==================\n$CMD\n"
eval $CMD

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / RDS / Quota Diagnosis
Technical Action Required: No (user action required to reduce file count)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium (job failing due to quota)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports `Disk quota exceeded` errors while writing files to an RDS project, despite not obviously expecting the project to be full. On RDS, this can happen either because the space quota is exhausted or because the inode/file-count quota has been reached. From the initial report alone, inode exhaustion is a strong possibility, especially given the shell errors creating temporary files, but `quota` output is needed to confirm whether the problem is space usage or file count.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1) Confirm which quota has been exhausted
   Ask the user to run:
     quota
   and check the relevant RDS project entry.
   - If the GB column is at or above the limit, this is a space-quota problem.
   - If the files column is at the limit or marked with an asterisk (*), this is an inode/file-count problem.

2) Explain the distinction between storage quota and inode quota
   - Storage quota = total disk space (GB/TB)
   - Inode quota = total number of files
   - Hitting either limit blocks new file creation

3) Provide remediation options

   Option A — Reduce file count (recommended if inode limit reached)
   - Delete unnecessary small files
   - Consolidate many small files into archives (e.g. tar)
   - Combine image files into fewer container formats if possible

   Example:
     tar -czf archive_episodes_12000_12100.tar.gz episode_0120*

   Option B — Increase storage allocation
   - Inode quota increases only when total RDS storage allocation increases.
   - This requires purchasing additional storage capacity.
   - Refer to RDS quota documentation.

4) Clarify policy
   - Inode quotas are fixed per TiB.
   - They cannot be increased independently of storage allocation.

5) Close once the user understands which limit was hit and how to proceed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Subject: Re: Quota exceeded on RDS project

Hello Liyou,

The `Disk quota exceeded` message can be caused either by running out of space or by reaching the inode/file-count limit on the RDS project.

Please run:

  quota

and send us the output for the affected RDS project. If the limit has been hit in the `files` column rather than the GB column, that would indicate an inode quota issue rather than lack of storage space.

If it is an inode limit, the usual remedies are to remove or consolidate many small files or to increase the total storage allocation.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not imply a filesystem fault without first confirming whether space or inode quota has been reached.
- Do not suggest modifying system quota settings directly.
- Inode quotas are proportional to purchased storage and are not adjustable independently.
- Avoid recommending unsafe bulk deletion commands.

### Default submission script for icelake[¶](#default-submission-script-for-icelake "Permalink to this headline")

You should find a symbolic link to an Icelake default job submission script modified for the icelake nodes in your home directory, called:

```
slurm_submit.peta4-icelake
```

This is set up for non-MPI jobs using icelake, but can be modified for
other types of job. If you prefer to modify your existing job scripts,
please see the following sections for guidance.

### Jobs not using MPI and requiring no more than 3370 MiB per cpu[¶](#jobs-not-using-mpi-and-requiring-no-more-than-3370-mib-per-cpu "Permalink to this headline")

In this case you should be able to simply specify the icelake partition to
the -p sbatch directive, e.g.:

```
#SBATCH -p icelake
```

will submit a job able to run on the first nodes available in the
icelake partition.

### Jobs requiring more than 3370 MiB per cpu[¶](#jobs-requiring-more-than-3370-mib-per-cpu "Permalink to this headline")

In the case of larger memory requirements, it is most efficient to submit
instead to the icelake-himem partition which will allocate 6760 MiB per cpu:

```
#SBATCH -p icelake-himem
```

If this amount of memory per cpu is insufficient you will need to
specify either the *–mem=* or *–cpus-per-task=* directive, in
addition to the *-p* directive, in order to make sure you have enough

Dear All,
I’m not sure if this is the correct address to use, I’m trying to purchase the following but Chemistry Stores are insisting on a quote, can you help please?
Cumulus CPU 200,000 hours * £0.01/CPU hour = £2000 PI is Prof Sir David Klenerman dk10012@cam.ac.uk
and
Research Data Store (RDS) 10TB for 3 years = £162/TB* 10 TB = £1620 PI is Prof Sir David Klenerman dk10012@cam.ac.uk
Regards
Dave
Mr David Pratt (he/him/his)
Senior Support Technician
Telephone Liaison Officier
Yusuf Hamied Department Of Chemistry,
University of Cambridge,
Lensfield Rd. Cambridge, CB2 1EW
Direct Line 01223 747611
Murphy's Law
If everything seems to be going well, you have obviously overlooked something.
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Procurement / Pricing Information
Technical HPC Action Required: No
Finance Action Required: No (information only)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is requesting a formal quote for:
- Cumulus CPU 200,000 hours at £0.01/CPU hour
- Research Data Store (RDS) 10 TB for 3 years at £162/TB

HPC Support does not issue formal quotes. Pricing information is published and departments must raise purchase orders via their own finance processes.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Clarify that formal quotes are not issued.
2) Confirm the published pricing model (if appropriate).
3) Advise that departmental finance teams should proceed using the published rates.
4) Do not generate bespoke quotation documents.
5) Do not raise purchase orders on behalf of departments.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: Quote request for Cumulus CPU and RDS

Dear Dave,

Thank you for your message.

We do not issue formal quotation documents for Cumulus CPU time or RDS storage. The services are charged according to the published pricing, and your departmental finance team can proceed based on those rates.

For reference:
- Cumulus CPU usage is charged per CPU hour at the published rate.
- RDS storage is charged per TB per year according to the current pricing schedule.

If your department requires confirmation of the published pricing, this is available on the HPC documentation pages.

Please let us know if you have any further questions.

Best regards,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not generate unofficial quote documents.
- Do not commit to pricing beyond the published rates.
- Do not raise purchase orders on behalf of users.
- Ensure any pricing referenced aligns with the current published documentation.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Purchasing / Quotes [1]
Technical Action Required: No [1]
Portal Action Required: Yes (Self Service) [2]
Urgency: Low [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is requesting a quote for purchasing compute hours on CSD3 and RDS storage [1]. The costs for these services can be found on the charges page [1][3]. 

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
Please accept this as an email quote for the PO that you are raising:
200,000 CPU hours at a unit rate of £0.01, total cost of £2000. 
10TB of RDS storage for 3 years at a unit rate of £54.00 per TB/year, total cost of £1620.
Owning PI is Prof. David Klenerman (dk10012).
Please ask your department to raise a purchase order to "Information Services", marking it clearly with your name, and to send the PO in PDF form by email to purchases@hpc.cam.ac.uk [1][5].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Please find below the quote for the requested services:
- 200,000 CPU hours on CSD3: £2000
- 10TB of RDS storage for 3 years: £1620
Please raise a purchase order and send it to purchases@hpc.cam.ac.uk.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- All costs are covered including capital depreciation of the machine, all running costs including power, management staff and support staff [2].
- The University-wide service is run as a self-sustaining cost centre and therefore must recover all costs incurred by the capital depreciation and running costs of the computer equipment [4].

[3] : HPCSSUP-73309

[5] : HPCSSUP-14219

## Introduction[¶](#introduction "Permalink to this headline")

The Research Computing Service aims to help deliver cutting edge research using innovative technology within the broad area of data-centric HPC. It is responsible for the hosting, system support, scientific support and service delivery of several large supercomputing and research data storage resources for the University of Cambridge research community.

The University-wide service is run as a self-sustaining cost centre and therefore must recover all costs incurred by the capital depreciation and running costs of the computer equipment, plus additional scientific support costs incurred to help increase the useful scientific output achieved from the machine. To this end the computational equipment within the Service is run as a Major Research Facility under the Full Economic Costing (fEC) funding model. Under this model, units of use are priced and research staff should determine how many units they require for a particular project, explicitly include these costs within a grant application for the project, and then pass this funding back to the HPC Service as a direct cost.

## Service Level 3 – Free Usage[¶](#service-level-3-free-usage "Permalink to this headline")

Service Level 3 (SL3) operates with the medium quality of service, QOS2. QOS2 is lower than QOS1 which is used in SL1 & SL2. This service level is designed for groups with medium usage requirements who currently do not have funding to pay for their usage, thus an immediate conversion of funds into credits is not required.

SL3 is capped with a maximum number of usage credits per PI per quarter. This has been introduced to promote a more even usage of the free time on the system. Currently each PI may receive **200,000 CPU core hours** and **3000 GPU hours** per quarter.

There is no guaranteed minimum usage level for SL3 and there is no concept of expiry or of moving core hours across quarters.

Once a group in SL3 has consumed all its allowed core hours in a quarter, jobs can still be submitted at Service Level 4.

## Migrating to CSD3[¶](#migrating-to-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

### Compute Services and Software[¶](#compute-services-and-software "Permalink to this headline")

It will be necessary for users to apply for new accounts on the CSD3
cluster, which can be done via [this form](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/access-csd3).

CSD3 gives all users an allocation of free usage, which is
operated at a Service Level (SL3) outlined here:
<https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/policies.html#service-levels>

CSD3 also offers paid-access, which operates under a simple pay-for-use
mechanism and gives access to priority Service Levels. University of
Cambridge users can see the following prices for paid access:
<https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/policies.html#costs-overview>

We are also making every effort to port over all necessary software
packages used on the WBIC platform to CSD3, so you can continue working
as before.

### Logging onto CSD3[¶](#logging-onto-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

As for the WBIC-HPHI system, login is via your University password but your target node is *login.hpc.cam.ac.uk* rather than the usual *wbic-gate* nodes. You should be able to set up graphical interfaces in a similar way as on WBIC-HPHI using X2Go or VNC. You will *not* need to set up the University VPN to access CSD3.

Please see the CSD3 user documentation, in particular:

and contact support@hpc.cam.ac.uk as before if you encounter problems.

user_input:
Dear HPC Team,
Could we add a few more recent CUDA modules to the cluster? At the moment I can load cuda/12.1, but many current DL packages (e.g., recent PyTorch/TensorFlow wheels) target CUDA 12.4 or 12.6. Having cuda/12.4 and cuda/12.6available would greatly improve compatibility and avoid building from source.
If feasible, installing these alongside the existing cuda/12.1 (plus matching cuDNN where applicable) would be ideal. I’m happy to test the new modules once they’re deployed.
Many thanks!
Best regards,
Yijiang Dong
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Stack / CUDA Module Availability
Technical Action Required: No (guidance only)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User requests CUDA 12.4/12.6 modules for package compatibility. CUDA 12.4 and 12.6 are not officially supported, and CUDA 12.8.1 is available in the Ampere test stack.

ACTION
1) Clarify that CUDA 12.4 and 12.6 are not currently supported.
2) Provide test stack commands for CUDA 12.8.1.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Yijiang,

We do not currently provide official CUDA 12.4 or 12.6 modules. We do, however, have a newer test software stack that includes CUDA 12.8.1.

Please run:

module purge
module load rhel8/slurm
module load rhel8/global
module use /usr/local/software/spack/csd3/spack-modules/test/ampere-2025-06-01/linux-rocky8-zen3
module load cuda/12.8.1/gcc/kdeps6ab

Please let us know if you run into any issues.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not promise unsupported module versions.
- Distinguish test stack from officially supported stack.

The user is requesting the addition of CUDA 12.4 and 12.6 modules to the cluster for improved compatibility with recent deep learning packages [1]. While there are existing tickets and documentation related to CUDA versions and module loading [1][2][3][4][5], it is unclear if the specific versions requested are available or can be made available without technical action.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Investigate the availability of CUDA 12.4 and 12.6 modules on the cluster.
2. If available, provide the user with the necessary module load commands.
3. If not available, consider escalating the request to the RSE team for potential installation [3].
4. Alternatively, suggest using a different approach such as loading a more recent version of CUDA using a specific module stack as shown in [1][3].

QUESTIONS TO ASSESS FEASIBILITY
- Are CUDA 12.4 and 12.6 compatible with the existing cluster hardware and software environment?
- Are there any known issues or limitations with loading these specific CUDA versions?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for your request. We are investigating the availability of CUDA 12.4 and 12.6 modules on the cluster. If these versions are not currently available, we may need to escalate your request for potential installation. In the meantime, you can try loading a more recent version of CUDA using the module stack suggested in our documentation [1]. Please let us know if you have any further questions or concerns.
Best regards,
HPC Support

- Do not install or modify software modules without proper testing and validation [1][2].
- Ensure that any new software installations are compatible with the existing cluster environment [1][3].

## Software[¶](#software "Permalink to this headline")

The PVC nodes run [Rocky Linux 9](https://rockylinux.org/), which is a rebuild of Red Hat
Enterprise Linux 9 (RHEL9). The Sapphire Rapids CPUs on these
nodes are also more modern and support newer instructions than most other CSD3
partitions.

To ensure your environment is clean and set up correctly for Dawn, please purge your modules and load the
base Dawn environment:

```
module purge
module load rhel9/default-dawn
```

As we provide a separate set of modules specifically for dawn nodes, in
general, we don’t support running software built for other CSD3 partitions on
Dawn nodes. Therefore you are strongly recommended to rebuild your software on
the Dawn nodes rather than try to run binaries previously compiled on CSD3.

Be aware that the software environment for Dawn is optimised for its hardware and
the binaries may fail to run on other CSD3 nodes, including cpu (*login-p*) and
icelake (*login-q*) login nodes. If you wish to recompile or test against this
new environment, we recommend requesting an interactive node with command:

```
sintr -t 01:00:00 -A YOURPROJECT-DAWN-GPU -p pvc9 -N 1 -c 24 --gres=gpu:1
```

The nodes are named according to the scheme *pvc-s-[1-256]*.

## Slurm partition[¶](#slurm-partition "Permalink to this headline")

The PVC (pvc-s) nodes are in a new **pvc9** Slurm partition.

Dawn Slurm projects follow the CSD3 naming convention for GPU projects and contain
units of GPU hours. User launched VNC on a login node and attempted local port forward, causing binding/forwarding issues. Correct method is to run VNC on an interactive GPU node (e.g. `*-r-gpu`) following documented workflow.

Hello Iryna,

Please create your TurboVNC session on an interactive GPU node (`*-r-gpu`) rather than a login node. That should resolve the forwarding issue you’re seeing.

Please follow the documented TurboVNC workflow for interactive GPU nodes, and let us know if you run into further issues.

Best regards,
HPC Support

- Do not encourage running long-lived GUI sessions on login nodes.

The recommended form of remote desktop uses the freely downloadable software [TurboVNC](http://www.turbovnc.org/). * Connection sharing doesn’t work with Filezilla and I keep seeing TOTP requests during file transfers.

## Connecting to an existing VNC session[¶](#connecting-to-an-existing-vnc-session "Permalink to this headline")

This subsection deals with connecting to a pre-existing VNC session from your local client machine. The client software can be downloaded from the [web site](http://www.turbovnc.org/) (.deb and .rpm files for Debian or RedHat-style Linux distributions respectively, .exe for MS Windows, .dmg for MacOSX). For example, to connect to VNC display :N on login-e-10 as user abc123, one might do from Linux:

```
/opt/TurboVNC/bin/vncviewer -via abc123@login-e-10.hpc.cam.ac.uk localhost:N
```

where this assumes that the TurboVNC executables have been installed in their default location. This should first prompt for the normal login password to establish an SSH connection to CSD3, then prompt for the VNC password. The VNC connection is being tunnelled automatically through the initial SSH connection.

In the 3D desktop case, the *localhost* in the above should be replaced by the name of the graphical node running the desktop, and the choice of login node in the specification of the tunnel becomes irrelevant.

Dear support team,
I need to rsync a large dataset from our rcs project (cc234). Doing it from a login node is probably not the way, as I'm being kicked out, I guess because the rsync is too heavy. Is there a dedicated data transfer node I can ssh to, or should I do it via an interactive job?
Thanks,
Yaniv
Sent from Outlook for Android<[https://aka.ms/AAb9ysg](https://aka.ms/AAb9ysg)>
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / RCS Data Transfer
Technical Action Required: No (best-practice guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User is running heavy `rsync` from login node and getting disconnected. Recommended approach is session persistence (screen/tmux) or GUI transfer workflow; follow RCS transfer best practices.

ACTION
1) Recommend using `screen`/`tmux` for long transfer sessions.
2) Optionally direct to supported GUI transfer methods for RCS.
3) Share RCS best-practice guidance.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Yaniv,

For long RCS transfers, please use a persistent terminal session (for example `screen`/`tmux`) so the transfer can continue if your SSH session drops.

You can also use a supported GUI transfer method:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rcs/gui.html

Best-practice guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rcs/best-practice.html

Best regards,

Hi,
We are having some issues with various ceph shares on the Arcus cloud 
today and yesterday. Seeing metadata servers switch to reconnecting e.g.:
> a900cf30-f8a3-42bf-98d6-af7ce92f1a1a.client1188705722/mds_sessions
> ::::::::::::::
> global_id 1188705722
> name "iris-gaia-code-spec-ro"
> mds.1 reconnecting
> a900cf30-f8a3-42bf-98d6-af7ce92f1a1a.client1188705717/mds_sessions
> ::::::::::::::
> global_id 1188705717
> name "process-space-1"
> mds.1 reconnecting
This is causing filesystem access to hang.
Rebooting the instances seems to be clearing this in some cases but it 
is happening on multiple instances and also recurring. Is there any 
indication of an underlying issue?
Cheers,
Patrick.
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / CephFS / MDS connectivity (Arcus cloud)
Technical Action Required: Yes (service-side investigation/mitigation)
Escalation Required: Yes (Ceph/storage administrators + network)
Urgency: High (filesystem hangs across multiple instances)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports Ceph shares on Arcus cloud hanging across multiple instances, with client session state showing repeated MDS reconnecting behaviour and filesystem access hanging. Because the issue affects multiple shares and instances, this is more consistent with a service-side CephFS/MDS or network-path problem than a single-instance misconfiguration. Rebooting instances may temporarily clear symptoms but should not be treated as the primary fix.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1) Acknowledge and treat as a service incident
   - Confirm the issue affects multiple instances/shares.
   - Prioritise service-side checks over detailed user-side debugging.

2) Collect minimal high-signal diagnostics from the user
   Ask for:
   - example affected instance name(s)
   - approximate timestamps of failures
   - exact mount command(s) or error output if readily available

3) Service-side investigation (storage/admin)
   Check:
   - Ceph cluster health summary and recent warnings
   - CephFS filesystem status and active/standby MDS state
   - MDS daemon logs around the failure window
   - client session reconnect/eviction behaviour
   - any ongoing rebalancing or recovery that could affect metadata availability

4) Network path verification (admin)
   Verify routing/interface configuration and any recent changes affecting CephFS traffic.

5) Ask users to retry only after backend checks or remediation have been completed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Patrick,

Thanks for the detailed report. Because the hangs are affecting multiple instances and shares, this looks more like a service-side CephFS issue than a single-instance configuration problem.

We’re investigating the backend now. In the meantime, could you please share:
- the names of one or two affected instances,
- the approximate timestamps of the most recent failures,
- and one example of the mount command or error output you are seeing?

Once we have checked the backend state, we can advise whether any instance-side action is still needed.

Cheers,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Treat multi-instance CephFS hangs as a service incident; avoid shifting burden to users.
- Avoid making claims about root cause unless supported by service-side evidence.
- Suggest disruptive workarounds (instance reboot) only with explicit user confirmation and after backend stability is restored.
- Do not request or expose sensitive infrastructure details in user-facing messages.

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / CephFS [1]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1]
Portal Action Required: No [1]
Urgency: Medium [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with CephFS shares on the Arcus cloud, with some shares failing to connect and others hanging [1]. The mds_sessions files show a mixture of "reconnecting", "open", and "opening" states [1]. This issue has been encountered before, and previous tickets suggest that MDS failovers may resolve the issue [1][3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Investigate the current state of the CephFS cluster and MDS servers [1].
2. Attempt to fail over the MDS servers to see if this resolves the issue [1][3].
3. If the issue persists, gather more information about the affected shares and the error messages being encountered [1].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more details about the error messages you are seeing when trying to access the CephFS shares?
- Have you tried rebooting the instances or failing over the MDS servers to resolve the issue?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
We are experiencing issues with our CephFS shares on the Arcus cloud. Some shares are failing to connect, while others are hanging. We have tried rebooting the instances, but the issue persists. The mds_sessions files show a mixture of "reconnecting", "open", and "opening" states. We would appreciate any assistance in resolving this issue.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- When troubleshooting CephFS issues, it is essential to investigate the current state of the cluster and MDS servers [1].
- MDS failovers should be attempted as a potential solution to resolve the issue [1][3].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-86663
[3] : HPCSSUP-87130

## Interconnect[¶](#interconnect "Permalink to this headline")

The CSD3 components are shown in the simplified topology diagram.
Only a few of the racks within the system, however it should be noted from a networking point of view each rack is composed of two 32 node leaves. Within this nodes are non blocking. To speed between racks the network is 2:1 in oversubscription. The GPU system differs in interconnect and uses Infiniband EDR. This is similarly 2:1 in its oversubscription, with 18 nodes per leaf switch. The GPU system accesses the Lustre storage via I/O nodes using LNET. These LNET routers do not show any performance reduction in benchmarks run both direct from the OPA attached compute nodes and the GPU system.

Dear support team,
Our team wanted to top up some more GPU core times and extra rds disk spaces for project 'QAKG-SL2-GPU'? Could you please give us a quote for the price of 10000 extra GPU hours and 20 GB extra rds space?
Best wishes,
Wenyu

CLASSIFICATION
Category: CORE Procurement / Quote Clarification
Technical Action Required: No (quote preparation and clarification)
Finance Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User is requesting pricing for 10,000 additional GPU hours and additional RDS storage on `QAKG-SL2-GPU`. Because the message says `20 GB`, which is unusually small in this procurement context, the safest response is to confirm the intended storage amount and whether the quote should cover only new storage or also any existing storage renewal/extension before issuing a final figure.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1) Clarify the storage request before quoting
   - Confirm whether `20 GB` is correct or a typo.
   - Confirm whether the user means additional capacity on top of the current licensed storage.
   - Confirm whether the quote should cover only extra storage or also renewal/extension of existing storage.

2) Quote the GPU hours separately using the current applicable rate.

3) Once the storage details are confirmed, issue a written email quote covering both the GPU hours and the corrected storage amount.

4) Ask the user to send the PO to the ticket once ready.

5) Keep any later account-creation request separate from the pricing calculation.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Dear Wenyu,

Before I finalise the storage part of the quote, could you please confirm whether `20 GB` is correct? That is unusually small for this service, so I want to make sure we quote the right storage amount and whether this is additional capacity or an extension of your current storage.

Once you confirm the intended storage quantity and scope, I can provide the full written quote for both storage and GPU hours.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not silently convert user-supplied units.
- If the storage quantity looks implausible, ask before quoting.
- Keep quote assumptions explicit, especially if renewal or extension of existing storage affects cost.

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is requesting a quote for 10,000 extra GPU hours and 20 GB extra RDS space for project 'QAKG-SL2-GPU' [1]. The cost of GPU hours is currently £0.55 per unit hour [3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Calculate the cost of 10,000 GPU hours: 10,000 * £0.55 = £5,500 [3].
2. Determine the cost of 20 GB extra RDS space. However, the cost of RDS space is not provided in the retrieved documents [1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8].
3. Provide a quote for the GPU hours and request further information on the RDS space cost.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- What is the cost of 20 GB extra RDS space?
- Is there a specific pricing page or document that outlines the costs for RDS space?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
The cost of 10,000 extra GPU hours is £5,500. However, we need further information on the cost of 20 GB extra RDS space. Could you please provide us with the pricing details for RDS space?
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- The cost of GPU hours is subject to change, and the current cost should be verified before providing a quote [3].
- The user should be directed to the relevant pricing page or document for the most up-to-date information on costs [4][6].

This lists the charge for each job in the CompHrs column. Since this example queries usage of a -CPU project, these are CPU core hours. Similarly, for a -GPU project they would be GPU hours.

**I would like to add core hours to a particular member of my group**

```
gdeposit -z 10000 -p halos-sl2-spqr1-gpu
```

This coordinator of the HALOS-SL2-GPU might use this to add 10000 GPU hours to the HALOS-SL2-SPQR1-GPU subproject assigned to the user spqr1. Note that if a compute hour limit applies to the parent of the project in the project hierarchy - i.e. if the parent project HALOS-SL2-GPU has an overall compute hour limit (which it almost certainly does) - then the global limit will still apply across all per-user projects.

Compute hours may be added to a project by a designated project coordinator user. Reducing the compute hours available to a project is also possible by adding a negative number of hours via the –time= syntax, e.g. the following command undoes the above:

```
gdeposit --time=-10000 -p halos-sl2-spqr1-gpu
```

## Service Level 3 – Free Usage[¶](#service-level-3-free-usage "Permalink to this headline")

Service Level 3 (SL3) operates with the medium quality of service, QOS2. QOS2 is lower than QOS1 which is used in SL1 & SL2. This service level is designed for groups with medium usage requirements who currently do not have funding to pay for their usage, thus an immediate conversion of funds into credits is not required.

SL3 is capped with a maximum number of usage credits per PI per quarter. This has been introduced to promote a more even usage of the free time on the system. Currently each PI may receive **200,000 CPU core hours** and **3000 GPU hours** per quarter.

There is no guaranteed minimum usage level for SL3 and there is no concept of expiry or of moving core hours across quarters.

Once a group in SL3 has consumed all its allowed core hours in a quarter, jobs can still be submitted at Service Level 4.

## Extending Access to CSD3[¶](#extending-access-to-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

It is the user’s responsibilty to ensure they have access to the service for the required time needed for their activity.
Users who are part of the University of Cambridge, who seek to extend their CSD3 account access past their initial university credentials expiry, will need to extend their student or visitor status with their university department. Doing so will extend their access to their university credentials and in turn, prolong access to the CSD3 service.
If a student is unable to extend their student status, they will need to register as a vistor with their department to maintain access to their university credentials for use with the CSD3 service.

For more on the University’s leaving procedure, please see this page: [help.uis.cam.ac.uk/service/accounts-passwords/leaving](https://help.uis.cam.ac.uk/service/accounts-passwords/leaving/)

user_input:
Hello,
I am having problems logging in to hpc via ssh irs38@login-gpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk as usual, even though an email has been sent out confirming the end of the maintenance work. I just wanted to check if there isn't something I am missing, or if it is just taking time until hpc will be fully available.
Kind regards,
Ieva
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Service Availability / Post-Maintenance
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports inability to SSH shortly after maintenance completion notice. Correct response is that services may still be coming online; direct users to service status and communications.

ACTION
1) Inform user service restoration is still in progress.
2) Provide status page and advise monitoring updates.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Ieva,

We are still bringing services online following maintenance. Please keep an eye on service communications and the status page for updates:
https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/service-status

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not declare full service restoration until confirmed.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Login / Access Issue [1][5]
Technical Action Required: Possibly [1]
Portal Action Required: No [2]
Urgency: Medium [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues logging in to the HPC via SSH, despite receiving an email confirming the end of maintenance work [1][5]. The user has tried logging in using their credentials and Microsoft 2-factor authenticator, but is still unable to access the HPC [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Check the HPC status page for any ongoing maintenance or issues [2].
2. Verify the user's account status and ensure that their credentials are correct [1].
3. Ask the user to try logging in again and provide any error messages they receive [1].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide the exact error message you receive when trying to log in?
- Have you tried logging in using a different method, such as the login-web interface [2]?
- Have you recently changed your password or updated your 2-factor authenticator settings [4]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for reaching out. I have tried logging in again, but I am still receiving the "Connection closed by" error message. I have not changed my password or updated my 2-factor authenticator settings recently.
Best regards,
Ieva

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the user's account is active and their credentials are correct before attempting to troubleshoot the issue [1].
- If the issue persists, consider escalating the ticket to a senior support specialist for further assistance [1].

## Frequently asked questions (FAQ)[¶](#frequently-asked-questions-faq "Permalink to this headline")

* I am unable to login to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk and now I get “Connection refused”.

  > + Make sure you are using the same userid, password or public key as you would to login.hpc.cam.ac.uk. As with the login nodes, failing to authenticate three times in succession to multi will cause your address to be blocked for 20 minutes, so if this happens please wait this long before trying again.
* I am using MS Authenticator but the TOTP code doesn’t work.

  > + If you still have the QR code and secret key on your screen then delete the account in your Authenticator and recreate it, making sure that the account is of the **Other** type (not *Personal* or *Work*). Otherwise please contact *support@hpc.cam.ac.uk* to request reset of the MFA secret before trying again, and select account type **Other**.
* When performing ssh to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk the QR code is replaced either by garbage or is broken up by blank lines, making it unusable.

  > + Setting **export LANG=en\_GB.UTF-8** in Mac terminals before performing the ssh may stop this happening - however if this has already happened you will need to contact *support@hpc.cam.ac.uk* to request reset of the MFA secret before trying again.

user_input:
Hi HPC Support Team,
I noticed that my home directory does not have a symlink ~/rfs pointing to the RFS project folder. Currently, I can access the project data using the absolute path /rfs/project/rfs-iCNyzSAaucw, but having the symlink would make it much more convenient.
Could you please create the ~/rfs symlink in my home directory?
Thank you so much for your help!
Best regards,
Hansheng
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Filesystem / Symlink / Home Quota
Technical Action Required: No (user guidance only)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User wants a convenience symlink from `~/rfs` to `/rfs/project/rfs-iCNyzSAaucw`. The correct command is straightforward, but the ticket shows the attempt failed because the user's `/home` directory was already over quota. In that case the symlink itself cannot be created until home usage is reduced.

ACTION
1) Ask the user to create the link with the concrete command:
   - `cd $HOME`
   - `ln -s /rfs/project/rfs-iCNyzSAaucw $HOME/rfs`
2) If they see `No space left on device`, ask for `quota` output.
3) Explain that the immediate problem is `/home` usage above the 50 GB quota, not an RFS permission problem.
4) Advise them to remove unneeded files from `/home` or move suitable data to `hpc-work`.
5) Mention that accidentally deleted files from `/home` may be recoverable from `~/.snapshot`.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Hansheng,

Please try:

  cd $HOME
  ln -s /rfs/project/rfs-iCNyzSAaucw $HOME/rfs

If you receive `No space left on device`, please run `quota` and send us the output. That would usually indicate your `/home` directory is full, which would prevent creation of even a small symlink entry.

If your home usage is over quota, you will need to move some data into `hpc-work` or delete unwanted files from `/home` before retrying. If you remove anything from `/home` by mistake, you can usually restore it from `~/.snapshot`.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Use the concrete path from the user request when it is already known.
- Distinguish `/home` quota exhaustion from RFS access problems.
- Do not advise deleting RFS data to fix a `/home` quota issue.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Symlink Creation
Technical Action Required: No [1]
Portal Action Required: No [1]
Urgency: Low [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is requesting a symlink to be created in their home directory to point to their RFS project folder, which is accessible via the absolute path /rfs/project/rfs-iCNyzSAaucw [1]. This is a self-service action that the user can perform themselves [1][5].

ACTION
Direct the user to create the symlink themselves by running the command `ln -s /rfs/project/rfs-iCNyzSAaucw rfs` in their home directory [1][5].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
You can create a symlink to your RFS project folder by running the command `ln -s /rfs/project/rfs-iCNyzSAaucw rfs` in your home directory [1][5]. This will create a shortcut to your project folder, making it easier to access.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Users should create symlinks themselves where possible [1][5].
- Support staff should not create symlinks manually unless absolutely necessary [1][5].

[1] : HPCSSUP-89742
[5] : HPCSSUP-89614

user_input:
hello,
after upgrading from gfortran-5.4 to gfortran-13.3 my fortran based mpi 
code produced interface related warnings during compile and crashed 
during execution. Replacing 'include mpif.h' statements with 'use mpi' 
statements (plus a few other changes) fixed the problems on my local 
linux box. However, when compiling on CSD3 I get the following mpi.mod 
error:
f951: Fatal Error: Reading module 
'/usr/local/software/spack/csd3/opt-2024-06-01/linux-rocky8-cascadelake/gcc-13.3.0/intel-oneapi-mpi-2021.12.1-cvatknvvaxe4ohzbsri6keicyjoy3w7f/mpi/2021.12/include/mpi/mpi.mod' 
at line 1 column 2: Unexpected EOF
The code is in /home/mh526/Atham/svn/branches/FlAshPlume. The compiler 
settings are in 
/home/mh526/Atham/svn/branches/FlAshPlume/source/Makefile.COMPILE
For the compile I load the following modules:
module load rhel8/cclake/base
module load gcc/13.3.0/7ukda7ns
module load netcdf-fortran-pnetcdf/4.6.1/gcc/intel-oneapi-mpi/ptuvn62k
The compile command  that produces the error is
/usr/local/software/spack/csd3/opt-2024-06-01/linux-rocky8-cascadelake/gcc-13.3.0/intel-oneapi-mpi-2021.12.1-cvatknvvaxe4ohzbsri6keicyjoy3w7f/mpi/2021.12/bin/mpif90 
-c -DMPI -g -O3 -ffree-line-length-none -mcmodel=medium 
-I/usr/local/software/spack/csd3/opt-2024-06-01/linux-rocky8-cascadelake/gcc-13.3.0/netcdf-fortran-4.6.1-ptuvn62k6et3sihhin2vrwbtyjuycp4o/include 
-I/usr/local/software/spack/csd3/opt-2024-06-01/linux-rocky8-cascadelake/gcc-13.3.0/parallel-netcdf-1.12.3-sn2y3yw32wdavsrvyy6g2dgru3x363d3/include 
/home/mh526/Atham/svn/branches/FlAshPlume/source/Basic/precision.F90
Are you able to tell me what the problem is? Am I using the wrong 
mpi.mod file?
Many thanks,
Michael
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Build/Compiler Toolchain / MPI Fortran Modules
Technical Action Required: Yes (workaround instruction)
Escalation Required: Optional (packaging improvement)
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
`gfortran 13.3` with Intel MPI hit `mpi.mod` read error (`Unexpected EOF`) because Intel MPI Fortran module files in that build were available only for older gfortran ABI versions. Workaround: explicitly include compatible `gfortran/11.1.0` (or `10.2.0`) MPI module path.

ACTION
1) Confirm exact module environment and minimal repro.
2) Explain ABI/module version mismatch.
3) Provide compile workaround with explicit include path.
4) Confirm successful compile with user.
5) Track packaging improvement for future stack.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Michael,

Please try compiling with an explicit MPI Fortran module include path:

mpif90 -I /usr/local/software/spack/csd3/opt-2024-06-01/linux-rocky8-cascadelake/gcc-13.3.0/intel-oneapi-mpi-2021.12.1-cvatknvvaxe4ohzbsri6keicyjoy3w7f/mpi/2021.12/include/mpi/gfortran/11.1.0 -c -DMPI precision.F90

(Using `.../gfortran/10.2.0` is also a valid workaround.)

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Be explicit this is a compatibility workaround, not ideal long-term packaging.

The user is experiencing issues with compiling a Fortran MPI code on CSD3, specifically with the mpi.mod file [1]. The error message "f951: Fatal Error: Reading module '/usr/local/software/spack/csd3/opt-2024-06-01/linux-rocky8-cascadelake/gcc-13.3.0/intel-oneapi-mpi-2021.12.1-cvatknvvaxe4ohzbsri6keicyjoy3w7f/mpi/2021.12/include/mpi/mpi.mod' at line 1 column 2: Unexpected EOF" suggests that the mpi.mod file is not compatible with the compiler being used [3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve this issue, the user should try loading a different MPI module that is compatible with their compiler, such as openmpi/gcc/9.3/4.0.4 [3]. Alternatively, they can try using a different compiler, such as gfortran, which is known to work with the mpi.mod file [2].

QUESTIONS TO ASSESS COMPATIBILITY
- What is the version of the compiler being used?
- What is the version of the MPI module being used?
- Has the user tried loading a different MPI module or using a different compiler?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Dear Michael,
To resolve the issue with compiling your Fortran MPI code, please try loading a different MPI module, such as openmpi/gcc/9.3/4.0.4, or using a different compiler, such as gfortran. If you are still experiencing issues, please provide more information about your compiler and MPI module versions.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Mixing .mods between different Fortran compilers can cause issues [5].
- Using the correct MPI module and compiler is crucial for successful compilation [1][6].

### GCC Compiler Suite[¶](#gcc-compiler-suite "Permalink to this headline")

The GCC compiler suite is accessed by loading the `gcc` module. For example:

```
module load gcc-7.2.0-gcc-4.8.5-pqn7o2k
```

Once you have loaded the module, the compilers are available as:

* `gfortran` - Fortran
* `gcc` - C
* `g++` - C++

### PGI Compiler Suite[¶](#pgi-compiler-suite "Permalink to this headline")

The Portland Group (PGI) compilers are available under the `pgi` modules. For example:

```
module load pgi/2018
```

Once you have loaded the module, the compilers are available as:

* `pgfortran` - Fortran
* `pgcc` - C
* `pg++` - C++

## Compiling MPI codes[¶](#compiling-mpi-codes "Permalink to this headline")

There are two prefered MPI libraries currently available on CSD3:

* Intel MPI
* OpenMPI

### Using Intel MPI[¶](#using-intel-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

To compile MPI code with Intel MPI, using any compiler, you must first load the
`intel/bundles/complib/2020.2` module (which on the cclake CPU nodes is
loaded by default as part of the `rhel7/default-ccl` module):

```
module load intel/bundles/complib/2020.2
```

## Compiling[¶](#compiling "Permalink to this headline")

Note that the `default-X` module, which is loaded by default on nodes of type X, arranges for `mpicc`, `mpif90` etc to be found and to use the recommended compilers and MPI implementation automatically when invoked. These wrapper commands supply the correct flags for compiling with the particular MPI implementation to which they belong.

When compiling code, it is usually possible to remove any direct MPI library references in your Makefile as mpicc & mpif90 will take care of these details. In the Makefile, simply set

```
CC=mpicc
```

etc, or define

```
export CC=mpicc
```

etc in the shell before compilation.

If some required libraries are missing, please let us know and we can try to install them centrally (as a module).

1.0}}, 'text': '# Modules Environment[¶](#modules-environment "Permalink to this headline")

Software on CSD3 is primarily controlled through the *modules* environment. By loading and switching modules you control the compilers, libraries and software available.

When compiling or running programs on CSD3 you will need to set up the correct modules, to load your compiler and any librarys that are requierd (e.g. numerical libraries, IO format libraries).

Additionally, if you are compiling parallel applications using MPI
(or SHMEM, etc.) then you will need to load one of the MPI environments
and use the appropriate compiler wrapper scripts.

By default, the rhel/default-{system-name} module is loaded, depending
on the login node you used, which loads a set of other modules.

Basic usage of the `module` command on CSD3 is covered below. For
full documentation please see:

**Note:** The modules provided by the [Spack](http://spack.readthedocs.io)
package manager behave differently to those usually encountered in Linux
environments. In particular, each module has the versions of dependency
libraries hardcoded using RPATH. More information is provided below. You
can identify Spack modules as they have a random string of 7 characters at
the end of their name, e.g.: `boost-1.66.0-intel-17.0.4-2xrjal4`.

## Using the modules environment[¶](#using-the-modules-environment "Permalink to this headline")

### Information on the available modules[¶](#information-on-the-available-modules "Permalink to this headline")

Hi,
Is it possible to run MPI-enabled processes on nodes?
[mf810@cpu-q-209 fasta]$ mpirun -n 1 ~/meme-mpi/bin/meme --help
MPI startup(): Warning: I_MPI_PMI_LIBRARY will be ignored since the hydra process manager was found
Abort(1090959) on node 0 (rank 0 in comm 0): Fatal error in PMPI_Init: Other MPI error, error stack:
MPIR_Init_thread(178)........:
MPID_Init(1532)..............:
MPIDI_OFI_mpi_init_hook(1474):
(unknown)(): Other MPI error
Thanks in advance!
Best regards,
Milena Flankova
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Job Execution / MPI under Slurm
Technical Action Required: No (user guidance with documented launch pattern)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User attempted direct `mpirun` and saw MPI startup/initialisation errors. For this environment, MPI jobs should be run inside a Slurm allocation using the documented submission pattern. Running directly from a login shell is a common cause of PMPI/launcher errors.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1) Confirm launch context
   - Ask whether command was run:
     - directly on a login node, or
     - inside a Slurm job allocation.

2) Provide documented MPI job pattern
   - Instruct user to submit via `sbatch` with account/partition/resources set.
   - Recommend launching tasks with `srun` inside the job script.

3) Provide minimal working template
```bash
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -A <ACCOUNT>
#SBATCH -p <PARTITION>
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#SBATCH --time=00:10:00

module purge
module load <required-modules>

srun ~/meme-mpi/bin/meme --help
```

4) If user needs interactive testing
   - First allocate resources interactively (documented method), then run with `srun` in that allocation.

5) Reference documentation
   - Share the MPI jobs section for the relevant partition.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Milena,

This error usually occurs when MPI is launched outside a proper Slurm allocation.

Please run your MPI command through a Slurm job script, for example:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -A <ACCOUNT>
#SBATCH -p <PARTITION>
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#SBATCH --time=00:10:00

module purge
module load <required-modules>

srun ~/meme-mpi/bin/meme --help
```

Submit with:

`sbatch <script_name>.sh`

Please also refer to:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/a100.html?highlight=mpirun#jobs-requiring-mpi

Using the documented Slurm launcher pattern should resolve this startup issue.

Regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Avoid ad-hoc launcher advice that bypasses Slurm resource allocation.
- Keep examples aligned with documented partition/account usage.

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with running MPI-enabled processes on nodes, specifically encountering an error with the Intel MPI Library [1]. The error message suggests a problem with the MPI initialization, which may be related to the version of the Intel MPI Library being used [1]. It is recommended to use the Intel MPI Library provided by the oneAPI toolkit [6] and to set the `I_MPI_OFFLOAD` environment variable to `1` for GPU-aware MPI [6].

ACTION
To resolve the issue, the user should try loading the Intel MPI module with the correct version, as recommended in [1] and [6]. The user should also ensure that the `I_MPI_OFFLOAD` environment variable is set to `1` in their submission script [6]. Additionally, the user can try specifying the full path to the `mpirun` executable, as suggested in [7].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more details about your submission script and the job you are trying to run?
- Have you tried loading the Intel MPI module with the correct version, as recommended in [1] and [6]?

Hello,
To run MPI-enabled processes on nodes, please ensure that you are using the correct version of the Intel MPI Library, as recommended in our documentation [1] and [6]. You can load the Intel MPI module with the correct version and set the `I_MPI_OFFLOAD` environment variable to `1` in your submission script [6]. If you encounter any issues, please try specifying the full path to the `mpirun` executable, as suggested in [7].

### Jobs requiring MPI[¶](#jobs-requiring-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

We currently recommend using the [Intel MPI Library](https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/tools/oneapi/mpi-library.html) provided by the oneAPI toolkit:

```
module av intel-oneapi-mpi
```

To use GPU Aware MPI and allow passing device buffers to MPI calls, set the `I_MPI_OFFLOAD`
environment variable to `1` in your submission script:

```
export I_MPI_OFFLOAD=1
```

If you are sure that your code only involves buffers of the same type (e.g.
only GPU buffers or only host buffers) in a single
MPI operation you can further optimize MPI communication between GPUs by setting

```
export I_MPI_OFFLOAD_SYMMETRIC=1
```

This will disable handling of MPI communication between GPU buffers and host buffers.

### Multithreading jobs[¶](#multithreading-jobs "Permalink to this headline")

If your code uses multithreading (e.g. host-based OpenMP), you will need to
specify the number of threads per process in your Slurm batch script using the
cores-per-task parameter. For example, to run a hybrid MPI-OpenMP application
using 24 processes and 4 threads per task:

```
#SBATCH -n 24 # or --ntasks
#SBATCH -c 4 # or --cores-per-task
```

If you do *not* specify the cores-per-task parameter Slurm will pin the
threads to the same core, reducing performance.

### Jobs requiring multiple nodes[¶](#jobs-requiring-multiple-nodes "Permalink to this headline")

Multi-node jobs need to request either exclusive access to the nodes, or 4 GPUs per node, i.e.:

```
#SBATCH --exclusive
```

or

```
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:4
```

### Jobs requiring MPI[¶](#jobs-requiring-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

We currently recommend using *the version of OpenMPI loaded by default
on the A100 nodes*, which is a version of OpenMPI configured for these
nodes. If you wish to recompile or test against this new environment,
we recommend requesting an [interactive node](#software).

For reference, the default environment on the A100 (gpu-q) nodes is provided by loading a module as follows:

```
module purge
module load rhel8/default-amp
```

However since the CPU type on gpu-q is different from any other on the
cluster, it is not recommended to build software intended to run on gpu-q on a
different flavour of node.

### Performance considerations for MPI jobs[¶](#performance-considerations-for-mpi-jobs "Permalink to this headline")

On systems with multiple GPUs and multiple NICs, such as Wilkes3 with 2x HDR NICs and 4x A100 GPUs per node, care should be taken to ensure that GPUs communicate with the closest NIC, to ensure maximum GPU-NIC throughput. Furthermore, each GPU should be assigned to its closest set of CPU cores (NUMA domain). This can be achieved by querying the topology of the machine you are running on (using nvidia-smi topo -m), and then instrumenting your MPI and/or run script to ensure correct placement. user_input:
Hi,
I have noticed that me and my colleague are unable to connect to the RCS. We both are connecting by SFTP, rcs.uis.cam.ac.uk on port 22 and both get the same unable to connect message.
We have also had a ticket from a user in our department explaining that she is also unable to connect where she was able to last week.
Is there a problem with the service at the moment? Or has anything changed that might be causing this?
Thanks
Kind Regards,
Shaun Cooke (he/him)
IT Service Manager
Department of Plant Sciences
University of Cambridge
Downing Street, Cambridge, CB2 3EA
Tel: 44 (0)1223 748962 | stc48@cam.ac.uk<[stc48@cam.ac.uk](mailto:stc48@cam.ac.uk)>
[https://www.plantsci.cam.ac.uk<https://www.plantsci.cam.ac.uk/](https://www.plantsci.cam.ac.uk%3Chttps://www.plantsci.cam.ac.uk/)>

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Access / RCS Connectivity / SSH-MFA
Technical Action Required: No (user onboarding guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports that multiple people are unable to connect to `rcs.uis.cam.ac.uk` over SFTP/SSH, raising the possibility of either a broader service problem or account-specific access changes. The correct first response is to confirm whether there is an active service incident and, if not, gather examples from affected users to determine whether the problem is shared or user-specific.

ACTION
1) Check whether there is an active service issue affecting RCS SSH/SFTP access.
2) Ask for example usernames, client/method used, and the exact error messages seen by affected users.
3) If the service is healthy, distinguish between users who cannot access RCS at all and users with account-specific denial errors.
4) For individual users, confirm SSH/storage MFA enrolment if relevant and ask them to raise their own tickets for account-specific checks.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Shaun,

We’re first checking whether there is any broader issue affecting RCS SSH/SFTP access.

If the service itself is healthy, we’ll need the affected usernames, the client or method being used, and the exact error messages so we can tell whether this is a shared problem or an account-specific one.

For users who can access the web portal but not SSH/SFTP services, we may also need to confirm that SSH/storage MFA has been set up separately.

If any individual user continues to have problems, please ask them to raise their own ticket so we can check their account details directly.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not troubleshoot third-party user accounts through one reporter without user-specific consent/context.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Connection Issue [1][3][5]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1][3][5]
Portal Action Required: No [1][3][5]
Urgency: Medium [1][3][5]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
There are reports of multiple users being unable to connect to the RCS using SFTP, with the error message indicating a connection issue [1][3][5]. The cause of the issue is not immediately clear from the provided information, but similar issues in the past have been related to network connectivity problems or authentication errors [1][3][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
Investigate the current status of the RCS service and check for any ongoing maintenance or issues that could be causing the connection problems [1][3][5]. If the issue is related to authentication, verify that the users are using the correct credentials and that their accounts are properly configured [2][7]. If the issue persists, consider escalating the problem to the storage team for further investigation [1][3][5].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more details about the error message you are receiving when trying to connect to the RCS?
- Have you tried connecting to the RCS from a different location or using a different network?
- Are there any other users in your department who are able to connect to the RCS successfully?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for reporting the issue with connecting to the RCS. We are currently investigating the cause of the problem and will provide an update as soon as possible. In the meantime, please try connecting to the RCS using a different method, such as the web interface, to see if the issue is specific to SFTP [1][3][5].

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- When troubleshooting connection issues, it is essential to verify that the user's credentials are correct and that their account is properly configured [2][7].
- If the issue is related to network connectivity, it may be necessary to involve the university's network support team to resolve the problem [1][3][5].

user_input:
Good morning, I am doing a benchmarking exercise on the ukaea-spr and ukaea-spr-hbm nodes.
I've managed to find most of the hardware info I need with some probing,
but for completeness I could do with some information as follows:
Network Interconnect
Network Topology
Storage Device Type (on the RDS filesystem) i.e. SSD/HDD/NVMe
Many Thanks in Advance,
Aleks
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: System Information / Hardware Specs
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User requested platform-level hardware details for benchmarking. Provided high-level interconnect/topology/storage architecture info.

ACTION
Provide requested specs:
- Interconnect: one Infiniband NDR 200 per node
- Topology: fat-tree
- Storage type: Lustre filesystem

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Aleks,

For `ukaea-spr-hbm`, the requested details are:
- Network interconnect: one Infiniband NDR 200 per node
- Network topology: fat tree
- Storage type (RDS): Lustre filesystem

Please let us know if you need anything further.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Keep responses at approved/public detail level.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Hardware / Benchmarking
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is conducting a benchmarking exercise on the ukaea-spr and ukaea-spr-hbm nodes and requires information on the network interconnect, network topology, and storage device type (on the RDS filesystem) [1][2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To provide the required information, we need to gather details on the hardware specifications of the ukaea-spr and ukaea-spr-hbm nodes. According to the documentation [2], the ukaea-spr-hbm nodes have High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) and are interconnected by Mellanox NDR200 Infiniband. The storage device type on the RDS filesystem is not explicitly stated in the provided documents.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more context about the benchmarking exercise you are conducting?
- Are there any specific requirements or constraints for the benchmarking exercise?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Aleks,
Thank you for your query. We are currently gathering information on the hardware specifications of the ukaea-spr and ukaea-spr-hbm nodes. According to our documentation [2], the ukaea-spr-hbm nodes have High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) and are interconnected by Mellanox NDR200 Infiniband. However, we need to verify the storage device type on the RDS filesystem. We will get back to you with more information.

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that all hardware specifications are verified through official documentation or support channels [1][2].

REFERENCE KEY

[2] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/sapphire-hbm.html::chunk::0000

{'docs': 1.0, 'tickets': 1.0}}, 'text': '# Sapphire Rapid Nodes with High Bandwidth Memory[¶](#sapphire-rapid-nodes-with-high-bandwidth-memory "Permalink to this headline")

*These new nodes entered general service in October 2024.*

This set of Sapphire Rapid Nodes have [High Bandwidth Memory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High_Bandwidth_Memory) built in and are optimised for applications requiring large data bandwidth.

**These nodes are for the exclusive use of the UKAEA (UK Atomic Energy Agency).**

## Slurm partitions[¶](#slurm-partitions "Permalink to this headline")

* These nodes are named according to the scheme *cpu-r-hbm-[1-84]*.
* These nodes are split equally between two Slurm partitions, **ukaea-spr-hbm** (nodes 1-42) and **ukaea-spr-hbm-flat** (nodes 43-84), corresponding to two memory configuration modes, *HBM-only* for the former and *Flat(1LM)* for the latter. See the [Intel configuration manual](https://cdrdv2-public.intel.com/787743/354227-intel-xeon-cpu-max-series-configuration-and-tuning-guide-rev3.pdf) for more details about these memory setups.

> Existing UKAEA-CPU projects will be able to submit jobs to both of these.

* These nodes have **112 cpus** (1 cpu = 1 core), and **9.1 GiB RAM per cpu** for a grand total of 1 TiB RAM per node. The nodes on the **-flat** partition have even more memory: **10.3 GiB RAM per CPU** for a grand total of 1.15 TiB RAM.
* These nodes are interconnected by Mellanox NDR200 Infiniband.
* These nodes are running [Rocky Linux 9](https://rockylinux.org/), which is a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 (RHEL9).

'tickets': 1.0}}, 'text': '# Sapphire Rapid Nodes[¶](#sapphire-rapid-nodes "Permalink to this headline")

*These new nodes entered general service in July 2023.*

## Slurm partitions[¶](#slurm-partitions "Permalink to this headline")

* The Sapphire Rapid nodes are named according to the scheme *cpu-r-[1-112]*.
* Some of the Sapphire Rapid nodes are in the **sapphire** Slurm partition. Your existing
  -CPU projects will be able to submit jobs to these.
* The Sapphire Rapid nodes have **112 cpus** (1 cpu = 1 core), and **4580 MiB RAM per cpu** for a total of 512 GB RAM per node.
* The Sapphire Rapid nodes are interconnected by Mellanox NDR200 Infiniband
* The Sapphire Rapid nodes are running [Rocky Linux 8](https://rockylinux.org/), which is a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8).

## Recommendations for running on Sapphire Rapids[¶](#recommendations-for-running-on-sapphire-rapids "Permalink to this headline")

Since the cpu-r nodes are running Rocky 8, you may need to recompile
your applications.

It is possible to log into a login node with compatible OS/software by logging into *login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk*,
which will land you on one of the *login-q-\** nodes.

Alternatively, request an interactive node using *sintr*:

```
sintr -t 4:0:0 -N1 -n38 -A YOURPROJECT-CPU -p sapphire
```

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2 and SL3
respectively.

The per-job, per-user cpu limits are now 4256 and 448 cpus for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

*These new nodes entered general service in October 2021 and were expanded in August 2023.*

## Slurm parition[¶](#slurm-parition "Permalink to this headline")

* The Ice Lake nodes are named according to the scheme *cpu-q-[1-544]*.
* The Ice Lake nodes are in separate **icelake** and **icelake-himem**
  Slurm partitions. Your existing
  -CPU projects will be able to submit jobs to these.
* The *icelake* nodes have **76 cpus** (1 cpu = 1 core), and 256 GiB
  of RAM. This means that they have **3370 MiB per cpu**.
* The *icelake-himem* nodes have **76 cpus** (1 cpu = 1 core), and 512
  GiB of RAM. This means that they have **6760 MiB per cpu**.
* The Ice Lake nodes are interconnected by Mellanox HDR200 Infiniband
* The Ice Lake nodes are running [Rocky Linux 8](https://rockylinux.org/), which is a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8).

## Recommendations for running on icelake[¶](#recommendations-for-running-on-icelake "Permalink to this headline")

Since the cpu-q nodes are running Rocky8, you will want to recompile
your applications.

It is possible to log into an Ice Lake login node with compatible OS/software by logging into *login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk*,
which will land you on one of the *login-q-\** nodes.

Alternatively, request an interactive node using *sintr*:

```
sintr -t 4:0:0 -N1 -n38 -A YOURPROJECT-CPU -p icelake
```

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2 and SL3
respectively.

Alternatively, request an interactive node using *sintr*:

```

```

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.
