Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
Peta4-CPU     - Intel Skylake/Omni-Path, Cascade Lake/IB
and Ice Lake/IB CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR IB GPU cluster.
Please note that currently:
Skylake and Cascade Lake use a RHEL7-compatible O/S
Ice Lake and Wilkes3 use a RHEL8-compatible O/S
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
This combination of RHEL7 and RHEL8 will become pure RHEL8 in time.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to either of the addresses below:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk will take you to one of a set of RHEL7,
OPA-enabled login nodes; these nodes are best suited to development
work for the RHEL7 Peta4 clusters. login-icelake will take you to a
different set of RHEL8, Mellanox OFED and CUDA-enabled login nodes
best suited for development work for Ice Lake and Wilkes3-GPU.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the best
snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a mixture of RHEL7 and RHEL8-compatible
operating systems. We recommend that all application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on skylake* and cclake* partitions, please use
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk (aka login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk).
For development on icelake* and ampere* partitions, please use
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk.
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. The module names contain hashes uniquely
identifying the build options and dependencies but the modules can be
searched and located using the spack command. E.g. to search for
modules providing HDF5 1.10.1 compiled using GCC, search for existing
builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C01%7Cga442%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C983f06fd84ac47a4a95108db9b9356d4%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638274829346491872%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C3000%7C%7C%7C&sdata=0%2FpMVyrjANtUCAgy6nDFjhYZ4m74kmynLUrCgbuJhdE%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C01%7Cga442%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C983f06fd84ac47a4a95108db9b9356d4%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638274829346491872%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C3000%7C%7C%7C&sdata=0%2FpMVyrjANtUCAgy6nDFjhYZ4m74kmynLUrCgbuJhdE%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses SLURM for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
SLURM submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Skylake
-------
Skylake jobs require the partitions skylake or skylake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p skylake
or
#SBATCH -p skylake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
skylake partition provides 1 cpu and 6020MiB of RAM per task, and the
skylake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 12060MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 6020MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the Skylake nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 32 cores in total.
Cascade Lake and Ice Lake
-------------------------
Cascade Lake and Ice Lake partitions work in a similar way with the following
differences:
The cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3420MiB per task by default.
The cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task by default.
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3380MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a
job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it
rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
2240 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,

### Step 2: Log into multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk[¶](#step-2-log-into-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk "Permalink to this headline")

Once you have connected, you may see a password prompt to log in.

![multi login - access](../_images/multi-login.png)

**University of Cambridge users**: You will need to enter your University password.

**External users**: You will need to use your SSH key or your password, depending on how your account was created.

If TOTP is already configured for your account, a message will be printed and the connection closed:

![multi login - TOTP already configured](../_images/multi-noop.png)

Otherwise, a QR code and secret key will be printed.

![multi login - QR generated](../_images/multi-qr.png)

*Please be careful not to point your camera at the example QR code displayed on the web page, as it will happily read it but this code is not valid for any purpose. You want to use* **your** *QR code from* **your** *login session to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk.*

Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
The O/S on CSD3 nodes is Rocky Linux 8, a rebuild of RHEL8,
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which
may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a
particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on
that type) you may alternatively SSH to
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to
reach nodes of that type.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system
but present additional development packages tuned to the node
hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees
for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options
and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the
spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1
compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cjb2421%40cam.ac.uk%7C82f7b6d03d904420a89e08de8e309829%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639104536533408285%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=NH%2F8TvUmdepzNnPbD3oQqKraOI1OcZKwSH4ANDOOC9Q%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cjb2421%40cam.ac.uk%7C82f7b6d03d904420a89e08de8e309829%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639104536533408285%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=NH%2F8TvUmdepzNnPbD3oQqKraOI1OcZKwSH4ANDOOC9Q%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by
a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if
it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,

Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
The O/S on CSD3 nodes is Rocky Linux 8, a rebuild of RHEL8,
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which
may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a
particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on
that type) you may alternatively SSH to
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to
reach nodes of that type.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system
but present additional development packages tuned to the node
hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees
for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options
and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the
spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1
compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cys695%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7Cbf8ecaf8ce464897f70708dd0fdc3c7a%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638684160804438417%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=%2FQWIy4mu7Bn%2BkIKkCzBQAJKMobQ8x5E7ecMnxog4SOk%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cys695%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7Cbf8ecaf8ce464897f70708dd0fdc3c7a%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638684160804438417%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=%2FQWIy4mu7Bn%2BkIKkCzBQAJKMobQ8x5E7ecMnxog4SOk%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by
a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if
it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,

When you are ready to configure TOTP, log into the special node *multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk*, via a terminal (not a browser). The sole function of this node is to set up TOTP if you currently have none configured.

ssh <username>@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

The authenticity of host 'multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk (128.232.222.63)' can't be established.

```
This host key is known by the following other names/addresses:
    ~/.ssh/known_hosts:18: login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
    (4 additional names omitted)
```

user_input: During my interactive session with RStudio, I noticed that if I am not active on the page while my model is running, I am logged out of the RStudio server, but the login I use for the HPC does not work to log back into the RStudio server. I have attempted to refresh the HPC main page, log out and back in, and then relaunch my RStudio session that is currently running. However, when I try to relaunch it, I am redirected to an RStudio login page that displays the message: “Temporary server error, please try again.”  When I attempt to log in using the same credentials I use for the HPC, it does not allow me to log back in. I have also tried Chrome and Safari, and seem to run into the same login page. My models still run, but I would like to be able to access the R page while they are running so I can see its progress. Thank you!
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: OnDemand / RStudio Session Reconnect
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is trying to reconnect to an already running RStudio interactive session after the web page timed out. In this situation, the RStudio login prompt may require session-specific connection credentials rather than the user’s normal HPC password. The next step is to retrieve the connection details from the running session page and use the session password shown there.

ACTION
1) Direct the user to the page for the running session.
2) Retrieve the session-specific connection details shown there, including the password.
3) Use that session password at the RStudio login prompt instead of the normal HPC password.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hi Julia,

Please open the page for the running RStudio session and retrieve the session-specific connection details shown there.

Use the password provided on that session page at the RStudio login prompt rather than your normal HPC password.

If you still cannot reconnect, please send us a screenshot of the session page and the login prompt.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with logging into RStudio Server on the HPC, receiving a "Temporary server error, please try again" message [8]. This issue has been reported by other users in the past, with similar error messages and difficulties connecting to RStudio Server [1], [5], [7].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve this issue, please try the following steps:
1. Ensure that you are using the correct login credentials and that your account is active [2].
2. Try restarting the RStudio session or launching a new one [1], [5].
3. If the issue persists, please provide more details about your setup and the exact error message you are receiving [8].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you please provide a screenshot of the error message you are receiving?
- Have you tried using a different browser or clearing your browser cache?
- Are you using any additional modules or custom settings in your RStudio session?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for reporting the issue with RStudio Server. We apologize for the inconvenience. To help us troubleshoot the issue, could you please try restarting the RStudio session and provide a screenshot of the error message you are receiving? If the issue persists, we will investigate further and provide additional assistance.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- When experiencing issues with RStudio Server, try restarting the session or launching a new one before seeking further assistance [1], [5].
- Ensure that you are using the correct login credentials and that your account is active [2].

To remove unwanted kernels from the Jupyter notebook:

   ```

user_input:
Dear support team
I am having trouble accessing the RFS service for my lab group (TP group, Condensed Matter Physics, Maxwell Centre). It has already been mapped, and it was working fine, but today it asked me for the password of my username (of the computer I was using). As you know, those are generated tokens. So, I generated a new one for the computer I am using, but it does not accept it. How can we fix that? It is urgent that I have access to my data.
Best regards
Patricia N. Lazo Paravicini
Pilizota Lab
Department of Physics
University of Cambridge
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: RFS Access / Credential Confusion
Technical Action Required: No (workflow clarification)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user was trying to use a generated token/device password for RFS access on Windows. The correct fix is to ensure they are on the Cambridge VPN or network, follow the Windows RFS instructions, and use their normal UIS credentials for Access Blue rather than the VPN/device token.

ACTION

user_input:
To Whom It May Concern,
For context, I have been using the Remote Desktop interactive sessions via the web interface for about a year.  As of this morning, requesting a desktop no longer provides the “Launch Remote Desktop” button.  Please see the attached screenshot. I expected to see the “Launch Remote Desktop” button as shown in the second screenshot. I would appreciate any insights or support. Thank you.
-Pete
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: OnDemand / Remote Desktop Launch Configuration
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The missing “Launch Remote Desktop” button can be caused by launch-form settings such as project or account selection, but the screenshots are needed to confirm the exact cause. The safest next step is to review the full launch settings and confirm which project or account is selected.

ACTION
1) Ask the user for a full screenshot of the launch settings if not already available.
2) Confirm that the correct project or account is selected in the Remote Desktop form.
3) If project selection is wrong, explain that the default project may not be appropriate.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Pete,

Please send us the full launch settings for the Remote Desktop session, including the selected project/account, so that we can confirm what is preventing the expected button from appearing.

If the current screenshot does not show the full launch form, please send one that includes the relevant project/account fields.

Best regards,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Login-Web / Remote Desktop [2]
Technical Action Required: Yes [5]
Portal Action Required: No [2]
Urgency: Medium [5]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing an issue with launching a remote desktop session through the web login, where the session queues but goes straight to "completed" without providing a "Launch Remote Desktop" button [5]. This issue has been persistent for the user [5]. Similar issues have been reported and resolved in the past, often related to configuration or partition issues [1][3][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Check the user's account configuration and partition associations [3].
2. Verify that the user has the necessary permissions and access to the remote desktop [1].
3. Investigate potential issues with the websockify process or other system logs [5].
4. Consider temporarily adjusting the user's quota or permissions to troubleshoot the issue [1].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more details about your account configuration and partition associations?
- Have you tried launching a remote desktop session on a different partition, such as "desktop" [7]?
- Are there any error messages or logs that you can provide to help troubleshoot the issue [5]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for reporting the issue with launching a remote desktop session. We will investigate this further and work to resolve the issue as soon as possible. In the meantime, please try launching a session on a different partition, such as "desktop", to see if the issue persists. If you have any further questions or concerns, please don't hesitate to reach out.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- When troubleshooting remote desktop issues, it's essential to verify the user's account configuration and permissions [1][3].
- Be cautious when adjusting user quotas or permissions, as this can have unintended consequences [1].

## startgfx[¶](#startgfx "Permalink to this headline")

The 3D visualisation-capable nodes are currently a set of specialised nodes each containing four NVIDIA L40 GPUs, 1TiB of RAM and 64 Ice Lake CPUs. This means that the remote desktop in this case is running on a non-login node, which changes some details of launching and connecting. Once connected to the remote desktop, usage proceeds as described above in [Using 3D graphics](#using-3d-graphics).

To simplify launching, locating and terminating 3D remote desktops, there are three commands which can be run from any login node:

```
startgfx

showgfx

killgfx nodename:displaynumber
```

As suggested by the names, *startgfx* will launch a new remote desktop on a random member of the 3D-capable set of nodes, and print out helpful information such how to connect to and how to terminate the desktop.

Note that if this is the first time VNC has been launched by the user, they will be prompted to set one or more VNC passwords, as described in [Starting a new VNC user_input:
Hello,
I hope you're well.
I'm a current PhD student and was just made a Data user on the Research Data Store for the Genetics_HPC partition by a collaborator. Upon opening the authentication link, I completely mistakenly clicked "Do not agree", rather than "Agree". The latter was certainly my intention, and now cannot access the drive.
Would there be any way to reverse this and reauthenticate?
Thank you,
Maria
Maria Jelen<[https://www.mrc-cbu.cam.ac.uk/people/maria.jelen/](https://www.mrc-cbu.cam.ac.uk/people/maria.jelen/)> (she/her)
Second-Year PhD Student at 4D Lab<[https://www.astlelab.com/](https://www.astlelab.com/)>
Medical Research Council Cognition and Brain Sciences Unit
mbj26@<[maria.jelen@](mailto:maria.jelen@)>cam.ac.uk
[cid:478d2978-d5c5-41c0-97e4-1b8771985808]
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage Access / Invitation Workflow
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user accidentally rejected the storage access invitation by clicking “Do not agree”. Helpdesk cannot reverse that state directly; the storage/data manager who originally added the user must re-add or re-invite them.

ACTION
1) Explain that the original invitation cannot simply be toggled back by helpdesk.
2) Direct the user to the Data Manager or project owner who granted access.
3) Ask that person to re-add/reinvite the user to the storage.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Maria,

In this case the Data Manager who added you to the storage will need to re-add or re-invite you. We cannot reverse that response directly from the helpdesk side.

Best regards,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Access Management [1]
Technical Action Required: Yes [6]
Portal Action Required: Yes [2]
Urgency: Medium [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has accidentally clicked "Do not agree" instead of "Agree" to the terms and conditions for accessing the Research Data Store, resulting in loss of access [1][4][6]. The solution involves re-adding the user by the Data Manager or PI [6].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
The Data Manager or PI should re-add the user to the project to re-initiate the access process [6]. The user should then receive a new invitation to accept the terms and conditions [2].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- What is the exact project name and ID that the user is trying to access?
- Has the Data Manager or PI attempted to re-add the user to the project?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Maria,
To resolve the issue with accessing the Research Data Store, please ask your Data Manager or PI to re-add you to the project. You will then receive a new invitation to accept the terms and conditions. If you have any further issues, please don't hesitate to contact us.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Users who have accidentally declined the terms and conditions should be re-added by the Data Manager or PI to regain access [6].
- It is essential to follow the proper procedures for managing user access to ensure data security and compliance [1][2].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-29506
[2] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/waldur-pi.html::chunk::0004
[4] : HPCSSUP-27304
[6] : HPCSSUP-23926

user_input:
Dear HPC cam support,
I am a new user that has been added to the project and i already set up my
MFA auth with MyAccessID and can access the dashboard of the project.
I have linked my ssh public key through the dashboard, although the mail
that i received says to send the public also to this email address. Thus, i
attach it just in case is needed.
ssh-ed25519
AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAIACYZwByA+c+iUfw7IQIy0u9my6yGu/FdXd1hnahrjYz
matteo.baldan.7@studenti.unipd.it
I got the message to "Select “Interactive Apps/Remote Desktop” and launch a
job on the pvc9 partition, requesting at least one GPU on one node, using
the above project.", but it seems that I am not able to find such
interactive apps and remote desktop..
I wanted to login with ssh access but I do not know which password would be
my credentials for the ssh access.
Thanks in advance, best regards,
Matteo
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: DAWN Onboarding / SSH Access / MFA
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has been added to a DAWN project but is unsure how to access the service and what credentials to use. The correct onboarding path is to confirm whether the submitted public key has been linked to the DAWN account, complete the separate SSH MFA setup, then connect using the corresponding private key and the DAWN login host.

ACTION
1) Confirm whether the public key submitted via the dashboard/email has been linked to the DAWN account.
2) Direct the user to the SSH MFA guide:
   https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk
3) Provide the DAWN login command:
   `ssh dn-bald1@login-dawn.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
4) Clarify that the SSH client must point to the private key paired with the public key they supplied.
5) Share the DAWN/PVC documentation:
   https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/pvc.html

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Matteo,

If your public key has already been linked to the DAWN account, the next step is to complete the separate SSH MFA setup and then connect using:
`ssh dn-bald1@login-dawn.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

If your SSH client asks for authentication, make sure it is using the private key that matches the public key you sent us.

Best regards,
HPC Support
